chapters.title: Introduction

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
Grammar of the Levantine Arabic variety

This article contains Levantine written in Arabic characters. Without proper rendering support, you may see Ш§ШӯЩҶШ§вҖҺ and Ш§ШӯЩҶШ§вҖҺ appearing as two different characters. If so, apply this custom style in your user settings:
[lang=apc] { font-family: 'Segoe UI', Tahoma; }
.

Levantine Arabic grammar is the set of rules by which Levantine Arabic creates statements, questions and commands. In many respects, it is quite similar to that of the other vernacular Arabic varieties.

chapters.title: Word order
chapters.summary: Both VSO (verb before subject before object) and SVO (subject before verb before object) word orders are possible in Levantine Arabic. The verb is before the object (VO).[1] However, Classical Arabic tends to prefer VSO, whereas in Levantine SVO is more common.[2] Subject-initial

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
Both VSO (verb before subject before object) and SVO (subject before verb before object) word orders are possible in Levantine Arabic. The verb is before the object (VO).[1] However, Classical Arabic tends to prefer VSO, whereas in Levantine SVO is more common.[2] Subject-initial order indicates topic-prominent sentences, while verb-initial order indicates subject-prominent sentences.[3]

In interrogative sentences, the interrogative particle comes first.[4]

chapters.title: Copula
chapters.summary: There is no copula used in the present tense in Levantine. In other tenses, the verb kДҒn (ЩғШ§ЩҶ) is used. Its present tense form is used in the future tense.[5]

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: There is no copula used in the present tense in Levantine. In other tenses, the verb kДҒn (ЩғШ§ЩҶ) is used. Its present tense form is used in the future tense.[5]

chapters.title: Definiteness
chapters.summary:
See also: Arabic definite article
There is no indefinite article in Levantine Arabic. Nouns (except proper nouns) are automatically indefinite by the absence of the definite article.[6]

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
See also: Arabic definite article
There is no indefinite article in Levantine Arabic. Nouns (except proper nouns) are automatically indefinite by the absence of the definite article.[6]

The Arabic definite article Ш§Щ„ (il) precedes the noun or adjective and has multiple pronunciations. Its vowel is dropped when the preceding word ends in a vowel. A helping vowel "e" is inserted if the following word begins with a consonant cluster.[7]

It assimilates with "Sun letters" - any consonant pronounced with the tip of the tongue. Other letters are called "Moon letters".[7] The letter Jeem (Ш¬) is a special case. It is usually a Sun letter for speakers pronouncing it as [К’] but not for those pronouncing it as [dНЎК’].[6][8]

sections.heading: Gender
sections.body: Nouns can be either masculine or feminine. In the singular, most feminine nouns end with TДҒКј marbЕ«б№ӯah (ЩҖШ©). This is pronounced as вҖ“a or -e depending on the preceding consonant. Generally, -a after guttural (Шӯ Ш® Ш№ Шә ЩӮ ЩҮ ШЎ) and emphatic consonants (Шұ Шө Ш¶ Ш· Шё), and -e after other consonants.[10]

sections.heading: Number
sections.body:
Nouns in Levantine can be singular, dual or plural.[11][10]

The dual is invariably formed with suffix -Д“n (ЩҠЩҶ-).[12][10] The dual is often used in a non-exact sense, especially in temporal and spatial nouns:

вҖҸШ¬Щ…Ш№Ш©вҖҺ, jumК•a, one week

вҖҸШ¬Щ…Ш№ШӘЩҠЩҶвҖҺ, jumК•atД“n, a couple of weeks (lit.вҖү'two weeks')[9]

For nouns referring to humans, the regular (also called sound) masculine plural is formed with the suffix -Д«n. The regular feminine plural is formed with -ДҒt. The masculine plural is used to refer to a group with both gender. However, there are many broken plurals (also called internal plurals),[13][10] in which the consonantal root of the singular is changed (nonconcatenative morphology). These plural patterns are shared with other varieties of Arabic and may also be applied to foreign borrowings: such as faЛҗtuЛҗra (plural: fwaЛҗtiЛҗr), from the Italian fattura, invoice.[9] The plural of loanwords may be sound or broken.[14] Several patterns of broken plurals exist and it is not possible to exactly predict them.[15]

Inanimate objects take feminine singular agreement in the plural, for verbs, attached pronouns, and adjectives.[16]

Some foreign words that designate weights and measures such as sОұnti (centimeter), ЕЎД“kel (shekel), and kД«lo (kilometer/kilogram) (but not mitr, meter, which behaves like other Arabic nouns) are invariable. The dual form is not used and numbers 3вҖ“10 don't lose their final vowel when followed by these nouns:

ЕЎД“kel: 1 shekel

tnД“n ЕЎД“kel: 2 shekels

talДҒte ЕЎД“kel: 3 shekels

К•aЕЎara ЕЎД“kel: 10 shekels[17]

sections.heading: Nominal sentences
sections.body:
Phrasal word order is head-dependent:[1]

Noun-Genitive

Noun-Adjective

Noun-Relative clause.

The genitive relationship is formed by putting the nouns next to each other,[18] this construct is called IбёҚДҒfah (lit.вҖү'addition'). The first noun is always indefinite. If an indefinite noun is added to a definite noun, it results in a new definite compound noun.[19][20][21]

Besides possessiveness, the IбёҚДҒfah construct can be used to specify or define the first term.[19]

Possession can also be expressed with ШӘШЁШ№, tabaC, especially for loanwords:

my dog: kalbi or il-kalb tabaCi,

the neighbors' house: bД“t il-jirбҫұn or il-bД“t tabaC il-jirбҫұn

your radio: ir-rбҫұdyo tabaCkom.[22]

There is no limit to the number of nouns that can be strung together in an IбёҚДҒfah. However, it is rare to have three or more words, except with very common or monosyllabic nouns.[18]

The IбёҚДҒfah construct is different from the noun-adjective structure. In an IбёҚДҒfah construct, the two nouns might be different in terms of their definiteness: the first is indefinite, the second is usually definite. Whereas adjectives always agree with nouns in definiteness.[23][19]

The first term must be in the construct state: if it ends in the feminine marker (/-ah/, or /-ih/), it changes to (/-at/, /-it/) in pronunciation (i.e. Ш© pronounced as "t"). Whereas in a noun-adjective string, the pronunciation would remain (/-ah/, /-ih/).[19]

sections.heading: Verbal nouns
sections.body:
Verbal nouns (also called gerunds or masdar[24]) play an important role in Levantine. Derived from a verb root, they can be used as a noun ("food") or as a gerund ("eating").[25] Verbal nouns do not exist as infinitives, they are not part of the verbal system but of the lexicon.[3]

chapters.summary: Number one and two have a masculine and feminine form. When used with a noun, they rather follow it like an adjective than precede it for emphasis.[27] An exception are uncountable nouns.[28] When the number 2 is accompanied by a noun, the dual form is usually used: waladД“n, 2 bo

sections.heading: Cardinal numbers
sections.body:
Number one and two have a masculine and feminine form. When used with a noun, they rather follow it like an adjective than precede it for emphasis.[27] An exception are uncountable nouns.[28] When the number 2 is accompanied by a noun, the dual form is usually used: waladД“n, 2 boys.[27]

Numbers larger than 3 do not have gender but may have two forms, one used before nouns and one used independently.[29] In particular, numbers between 3 and 10 lose their final vowel before a noun.[27]

Numbers from 3 to 10 are followed by plural nouns. Numbers from 11 to 99 are followed by a singular.[29][30][27]

Numbers 100 and onwards follow the same rule as numbers 0вҖ“99 based on their last two digits. 100 and 101 are followed by a singular, 102 is followed by a dual (102 books: miyye u-ktДҒbД“n), 103вҖ“110 by a plural, and 111вҖ“199 is like 11вҖ“99, followed by a singular.[31]

Before a small set of nouns (e.g. ШЈЩ„ЩҒ, Кҫalf, "thousand") the independent form is used in construct state (Ш© pronounced as "t"). Щ…ЩҠШ© (miyye, "hundred") is always in construct state before nouns.[28]

sections.heading: Ordinal numbers and fractions
sections.body:
Ordinal numbers can either precede or follow the noun. If they precede the noun the masculine form is used and the definite article is dropped.[28]

Ordinal numbers above 10 do not exist, instead the cardinal numbers are used following the noun.[28]

chapters.title: Adjectives
chapters.summary: Many adjectives have the pattern ЩҒШ№ЩҠЩ„ (fК•Д«l / CCД«C or faК•Д«l / CaCД«C) but other patterns are also possible.[20]

sections.heading: Form
sections.body:
Many adjectives have the pattern ЩҒШ№ЩҠЩ„ (fК•Д«l / CCД«C or faК•Д«l / CaCД«C) but other patterns are also possible.[20]

Adjectives derived from nouns by the suffix ЩҖЩҠ (-i) are called Nisba adjectives. Their feminine form ends in ЩҖЩҠШ© (-iyye) and the plural in ЩҖЩҠЩҠЩҶ (-iyyД«n).[32]

sections.heading: Gender
sections.body: Adjectives typically have three form: a masculine singular, a feminine singular, and a plural which does not distinguish gender. In most adjectives the feminine is formed through addition of -a/e, sometimes dropping an unstressed short vowel.[33]

sections.heading: Number
sections.body:
Nouns in dual have adjectives in plural.[20]

The plural of adjectives is either regular ending in ЩҖЩҠЩҶ (-Д«n) or is an irregular "broken" plural. It is used with nouns referring to people. For non-human / inanimate / abstract nouns, adjectives can use either the plural or the singular feminine form regardless of the noun's gender.[33][20][34][16]
examples: The plural of adjectives is either regular ending in ЩҖЩҠЩҶ (-Д«n) or is an irregular "broken" plural. It is used with nouns referring to people. For non-human / inanimate / abstract nouns, adjectives can use either the plural or the singular f

sections.heading: Word order
sections.body:
Adjectives follow the noun they modify and agree with it in definiteness. Adjectives without an article after a definite noun express a clause with the invisible copula "to be".[35]

Examples

ШЁЩҠШӘ ЩғШЁЩҠШұ bД“t kbД«r
a big house

Ш§Щ„ШЁЩҠШӘ Ш§Щ„ЩғШЁЩҠШұ il-bД“t le-kbД«r
the big house

Ш§Щ„ШЁЩҠШӘ ЩғШЁЩҠШұ il-bД“t kbД«r
the house is big

There is no dominant order for degree words and adjectives: Adverbs of degree like вҖҸЩғШӘЩҠШұвҖҺ (ktД«r, "very") and вҖҸШҙЩҲЩҠвҖҺ (ЕЎwayy, "a little / a bit") can either precede or follow the adjective.[1]
examples: Adjectives follow the noun they modify and agree with it in definiteness. Adjectives without an article after a definite noun express a clause with the invisible copula "to be".[35]
Examples

There is no dominant order for degree words and adjectives: Adverbs of degree like вҖҸЩғШӘЩҠШұвҖҺ (ktД«r, "very") and вҖҸШҙЩҲЩҠвҖҺ (ЕЎwayy, "a little / a bit") can either precede or follow the adjective.[1]

sections.heading: Superlative and comparative
sections.body:
There are no separate comparative and superlative forms but the elative is used in both cases.[33]

The elative is formed by adding a hamza at the beginning of the adjective and replace the vowels by "a" (pattern: ШЈЩҒШ№Щ„ КҫafК•al / aCCaC).[20] Adjective endings in вҖҸЩҠвҖҺ (i) and вҖҸЩҲвҖҺ (u) are changed into вҖҸЫҢвҖҺ (a). If the second and third consonant in the root are the same, they are geminated (pattern: ШЈЩҒЩ„Щ‘ Кҫafall / КҫaCaCC).[36]

Speakers who pronounce вҖҸЩӮвҖҺ as hamza might pronounced the elative prefix as "h" in order to avoid two consecutive hamzas.[37]

When an elative modifies a noun, it precedes the noun an no definite article is used.[38]

In order to compare two things, the word вҖҸЩ…ЩҶвҖҺ (min, lit.вҖү'from') is used in the sense of "than" in English.[38]

chapters.title: Pronouns
chapters.summary: Feminine plural forms modifying human females are found mostly in rural and Bedouin areas. They are not mentioned below.[39]

sections.heading: Personal pronouns
sections.body:
Levantine has eight persons, and therefore eight pronouns. Dual forms that exist in Modern Standard Arabic do not exist in Levantine, the plural is used instead. Because conjugated verbs indicate the subject with a prefix and/or a suffix, independent subject pronouns are usually not necessary and are mainly used for emphasis.[40][41]

Indirect object pronouns
Indirect object pronouns (dative) are suffixed to the conjugated verb. They are form by adding an Щ„ (-l) and then the possessive suffix to the verb.[39] They precede object pronouns if present:

jДҒb-ha la-К”abЕ«y: he brought it to my father,

jab-lo il-jarД«de: he brought him the newspaper,

jab-lo yyДҒ-ha: he brought him it.[39][45]

sections.heading: Demonstrative pronouns
sections.body:
Demonstrative pronouns have three referential types: immediate, proximal, and distal. The distinction between proximal and distal demonstratives is of physical, temporal, or metaphorical distance. The genderless and numberless immediate demonstrative article вҖҸЩҮШ§вҖҺ ha is translated by "this/the", to designate something immediately visible or accessible.[46]

sections.heading: Root
sections.body:
Like Arabic verbs, most Levantine verbs are based on a triliteral root (also called radical) made of three consonants (therefore also called triconsonantal root). The set of consonants communicates the basic meaning of a verb, e.g. k-t-b 'write', q-r-вҖҷ 'read', вҖҷ-k-l 'eat'. Changes to the vowels in between the consonants, along with prefixes or suffixes, specify grammatical functions such as tense, person and number, in addition to changes in the meaning of the verb that embody grammatical concepts such as mood (e.g. indicative, subjunctive, imperative), voice (active or passive), and functions such as causative, intensive, or reflexive.[48]

Quadriliteral roots are less common, but often used to coin new vocabulary or to Arabicize foreign words.[49][50]

The base form is the third-person masculine singular of the perfect (also called past) tense.[51]

sections.heading: Verb forms
sections.body:
For broader coverage of this topic, see Wiktionary:Template:ajp-conj/documentation.
Almost all Levantine verbs can be categorized in one of ten verb forms (also called verb measures,[52] stems,[53] patterns,[54] or types[55]). Form I, the most common one, serves as a base for the other nine forms. Each form carries a different verbal idea, relative to the meaning of its root. Technically, 10 verbs can be constructed from any given triconsonantal root. However, all of those ten forms may not be used in practice by speakers.[48] After Form I, Forms II, V, VII, and X are the most common ones.[53]

In addition to its form, each verb has a "quality":

Sound (or regular): 3 distinct radicals, neither the second nor the third is w or y,

Verbs containing the radicals w or y are called weak. They can be either:
Hollow: verbs with w or y as the second radical, which can become a long a in some forms, or

Defective: verbs with w or y as the third radical, treated as a vowel,

Geminate (or doubled): the second and third radicals are identical, remaining together as a double consonant.[52]

Some irregular verbs do not fit into any of the verb forms.[52]

The initial i in verb forms VII, VIII, IX, X drops when the preceding word ends in a vowel or at the beginning of a sentence.[7]

sections.heading: Regular verb conjugation
sections.body:
The Levantine verb has only two tenses: past (perfect) and present (also called imperfect, b-imperfect, or bi-imperfect). The future tense is an extension of the present tense. The negative imperative is the same as the negative present with helping verb (imperfect). The grammatical person and number as well as the mood are designated by a variety of prefixes and suffixes. The following table shows the paradigm of a sound Form I verb, katab (ЩғШӘШЁ) 'to write'.[48]

The b-imperfect is usually used for the indicative mood (non-past present, habitual/general present, narrative present, planned future actions, or potential). The prefix b- is deleted in the subjunctive mood, usually after various modal verbs, auxiliary verbs, pseudo-verbs, prepositions, and particles.[20][56][57][58]

In the following table, the accented vowel is in bold.

sections.heading: Active participle
sections.body:
The active participle, also called present participle, is grammatically an adjective derived from a verb. Depending on the context, it can express the present or present continuous (with verbs of motion, location, or mental state), the near future, or the present perfect (past action with a present result).[67] It can also serve as a noun or an adjective.[68]

The active participle can be inflected from the verb based on its verb form.[68]

sections.heading: Passive participle
sections.body:
The passive participle, also called past participle,[24] has a similar meaning as in English (i.e. sent, written, etc.). It is mostly used as an adjective but it can sometimes be used as a noun. It is inflected from the verb based on its verb form.[79] However, in practice, passive participles are largely limited to verb forms I (CvCvC) and II (CvCCvC), becoming maCCЕ«C for the former and mCaCCaC for the latter.[3]

sections.heading: Future
sections.body:
There are various ways to express the future. One is by using the present tense (with b- prefix) on its own. Another one is by using вҖҸШЁШҜвҖҺ (bidd-, lit.вҖү'want').[80]

The future tense is formed with the imperfect preceded by the particle вҖҸШұШӯвҖҺ (raбёҘ) or by the prefixed particle вҖҸШӯЩҖвҖҺ (бёҘa-).[81]

sections.heading: To have
sections.body:
Levantine does not have a verb "to have". Instead, possession is expressed using the prepositions Ш№ЩҶШҜ (К•ind, lit.вҖү'at', meaning "to possess") and Щ…Ш№ (maК•, lit.вҖү'with', meaning "to have on oneself"), followed by personal pronoun suffixes. The past indicator ken and the future indicator raH are used to express possession in the past or the future, respectively.[90][91]

chapters.title: Adverbs
chapters.summary: Levant does not distinguish between adverbs and adjectives in adverbial function. Almost any adjective can be used as an adverb: вҖҸЩ…ЩҶЩҠШӯвҖҺ (mnД«бёҘ, вҖҳgoodвҖҷ) vs. ЩҶЩ…ШӘЩҠ Щ…ЩҶЩҠШӯШҹ (nimti mnД«бёҘ, вҖҳDid you sleep well?вҖҷ) Adverbs from MSA, showing the suffix -an, are often used, e.g. вҖҸШЈШЁШҜШ§вҖҺ (Кҫabadan

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
Levant does not distinguish between adverbs and adjectives in adverbial function. Almost any adjective can be used as an adverb: вҖҸЩ…ЩҶЩҠШӯвҖҺ (mnД«бёҘ, вҖҳgoodвҖҷ) vs. ЩҶЩ…ШӘЩҠ Щ…ЩҶЩҠШӯШҹ (nimti mnД«бёҘ, вҖҳDid you sleep well?вҖҷ) Adverbs from MSA, showing the suffix -an, are often used, e.g. вҖҸШЈШЁШҜШ§вҖҺ (Кҫabadan, вҖҳat allвҖҷ).[3] Adverbs often appear after the verb or the adjective. вҖҸЩғШӘЩҠШұвҖҺ (ktД«r, вҖҳveryвҖҷ) can be positioned after or before the adjective.[3]

Adverbs of manner can usually be formed using bi- followed by the nominal form: вҖҸШЁШіШұШ№Ш©вҖҺ (b-sirКҝa, вҖҳfast, quicklyвҖҷ, lit.вҖү'with speed').[57]

chapters.title: Subordination
chapters.summary: Relative clauses are formed with the particle yalli/illi/halli (the one who) when definite things are being described. It can be used either for people (who) or objects (that, which).[94][95][96]

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
Relative clauses are formed with the particle yalli/illi/halli (the one who) when definite things are being described. It can be used either for people (who) or objects (that, which).[94][95][96]

If the noun to which the relative pronoun refers is indefinite and non specific, the relative clause is linked without any coordinating conjunction and is indistinguishable from an independent sentence.[97][95][96]

Examples of relative clauses[96][58]

English
Levantine (Arabic)
Levantine (Latin)
Note

I saw the boy who was playing football.
ШҙЩҒШӘ Ш§Щ„ЩҲЩ„ШҜ Ш§Щ„Щ„ЩҠ ЩғШ§ЩҶ ЩҠЩ„Ш№ШЁ ЩҒШ·ШЁЩҲЩ„
ЕЎuft il-walad illi kДҒn yilК•ab faб№ӯbЕҚl.
Definite subject: use of illi

I saw a girl playing football.
ШҙЩҒШӘ ШЁЩҶШӘ ЩғШ§ЩҶШӘ ШӘЩ„Ш№ШЁ ЩҒШ·ШЁЩҲЩ„
ЕЎuft bint kДҒnat tilК•ab faб№ӯbЕҚl.
Indefinite subject: sentences connected without a pronoun

In formal speech, sentence complements can be introduced with the particle К”Зқnn ("that"), to which some speakers attach a personal pronoun (o or i).[97]

For circumstantial clauses, the conjunction w- introduces subordinate clauses with the sense "while, when, with".[98]

Temporal adverbs such as baК•d (after) may be used with the "ma" to form a subordinate clause: baК•d ma tnaЛҗm ("after she goes to sleep").[97]
